docs.text:
• Universal Fit: Avatars have different head shapes. Design hats that are slightly loose or have adjustable straps (visually) to minimize clipping.
  • Hair Interaction: Large hats often clip with hair.
    • Advanced: You can set the hides_hair property to true in the resource. This tells the Avatar system to disable the hair mesh when this hat is equipped.

docs.title: Demo Workout Package Guide
docs.text: This guide points to one concrete example of the current AeroBeat workout-package contract.

docs.title: Where the example lives
docs.text: • docs/examples/workout-packages/demo-neon-boxing-bootcamp/

docs.title: What to open first
docs.text:
1. workout.yaml
  2. Set records:
  3. ab-set-neon-stride-opening-round.yaml
  4. ab-set-neon-stride-flow-round.yaml
  5. ab-set-midnight-sprint-finish-round.yaml
  6. Song records
  7. Chart records:
  8. Boxing
  9. Flow
  10. coaches/coach-config.yaml
  11. Environment records
  12. SQL schema examples

docs.title: 1. One self-contained workout package
docs.text:
The example models a single package with:

  • one root workout.yaml
  • two songs
  • three charts
  • three sets
  • one coaching domain file
  • two environments
  • example SQL schema files

docs.title: 2. Exact ids, not loose lookup rules
docs.text:
The set files point to exact ids for:

  • songId
  • chartId
  • environmentId
  • coachingOverlayId

Discovery happens elsewhere; package playback resolves exact ids.

docs.title: 3. Coaching stays package-local
docs.text:
• every package has exactly one coaches/coach-config.yaml
  • coaching is optional all-or-nothing
  • enabled coaching owns warmup/cooldown media and the overlay registry
  • sets choose overlay clips by coachingOverlayId

docs.title: 4. Environments remain part of the package model
docs.text:
The checked-in environment examples teach the small Environment v1 record shape:

  • environmentId
  • environmentName
  • type
  • resourcePath

docs.title: 5. What is intentionally gone from this example
docs.text:
This docs pass removes the older package-local gameplay asset concept.

If you are looking for long-term customization direction, think in terms of:

  • avatar identity
  • cosmetics
  • workout-point unlock paths

not freeform package-local asset swapping inside every workout.

docs.title: Creating Environments
docs.text:
Environments remain part of the official workout-package concept.

Scope note: this page describes the deeper Godot-authored environment workflow, which should still be treated as an advanced controlled-pipeline path. The baseline package contract stays smaller: package YAML should point at image_background, video_background, or cleaned glb_environment payloads via type + resourcePath.

docs.title: Why environments stay
docs.text: The current product slice keeps environments because they meaningfully shape the workout atmosphere without reopening the older package-asset customization model.

docs.title: Environment goals
docs.text:
• readable workout presentation
  • good performance on the PC-first community target
  • future portability to mobile and later VR where feasible

docs.title: Current authoring guidance
docs.text:
• prefer clean, lightweight authored backgrounds or GLB environments
  • optimize for readability first, spectacle second
  • keep runtime assumptions conservative for the first community release
  • avoid teaching loose godot_scene as the default community handoff

docs.title: Product note
docs.text: If you are looking for customization beyond environments, the likely future direction is controlled avatar/cosmetics unlocks rather than package-local gameplay asset bundles.

docs.title: Feature Development Guide
docs.text: This guide is for gameplay engineers building AeroBeat features.

docs.title: Current product focus
docs.text:
The active product slice is centered on:

  • Boxing
  • Flow
  • camera-first gameplay

The architecture may still support future features later, but feature-development docs should not imply Dance or Step are active product modes today.

docs.title: What a feature owns
docs.text:
A feature is a self-contained library that provides:

  1. gameplay logic
  2. stable runtime/view-facing scenes or resources
  3. choreography parsing for that feature's authored charts

It does not provide main-menu UI, hardware input providers, or player-profile storage.

docs.title: Stability rule
docs.text: Feature-facing contracts should stay stable enough that downstream visuals and tooling do not break when implementation details change.

docs.title: Modding Quickstart Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the AeroBeat creator community.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
Read the Demo Workout Package Guide first. It walks through the current package contract built around:

  • workout.yaml
  • songs/
  • charts/
  • sets/
  • coaches/coach-config.yaml
  • environments/
  • example SQL schemas

docs.title: Creator lanes in this slice
docs.text:
• musicians create songs
  • choreographers create Boxing and Flow charts
  • workout authors compose sets and workouts
  • coaches create optional coaching content
  • environment authors create atmosphere for workouts

docs.title: Customization note
docs.text: Older docs talked more heavily about package-local gameplay skins/assets. That is no longer the main package story in this slice. Future customization is more likely to live in controlled avatar/cosmetics systems tied to progression.

docs.title: Creating Custom Skins
docs.text: In AeroBeat, "Skins" allow athletes to personalize their gameplay equipment. This guide covers creating custom Boxing Gloves, Flow Bats, Targets, and Obstacles.

docs.title: 🛠️ The Skins SDK
docs.text:
• SDK: aerobeat-skins-* (Template: skins)
  • Tools: Godot 4.x Editor.
  • Output: An AeroSkin resource packed into a .pck.

docs.title: 🥊 Boxing Gloves
docs.text: Gloves are the athlete's primary connection to the game. They must look good from the "First Person" perspective.

docs.title: Technical Constraints
docs.text:
• Polycount: < 10k triangles per glove.
  • Orientation: Fingers point Negative Z. Palm faces Negative Y (Down).
  • Materials: Standard PBR. Avoid transparent materials for performance.

docs.title: The Left/Right Rule
docs.text:
AeroBeat relies on color coding for gameplay.

  • Left Hand: Must be predominantly Dark / Black.
  • Right Hand: Must be predominantly Light / White.
  • Why? Players instinctively match the glove color to the target color. If you make a "Red vs Blue" set, players might get confused hitting Black/White targets.

docs.title: ⚔️ Flow Bats
docs.text: Bats are the primary tool for Flow Mode. They are used for wide, sweeping strikes that engage the core and shoulders.

docs.title: Technical Constraints
docs.text:
• Polycount: < 10k triangles per bat.
  • Orientation: Handle grip is centered at (0,0,0). The blade extends along Negative Z (Forward).
  • Length: The visual mesh should be approximately 0.8m to 1.0m long.

docs.title: The Left/Right Rule
docs.text: Just like gloves, bats must follow the strict color coding: * Left Bat: Predominantly Dark / Black. * Right Bat: Predominantly Light / White.

docs.title: 🎯 Targets
docs.text: Targets are the objects flying at the player.

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
1. Directional Target: Has a clear "Front" face. Used for punches.
  2. Omni Target: Symmetrical. Used for "Any Direction" hits.
  3. Obstacle: Walls or shapes to dodge.

docs.title: Technical Constraints
docs.text:
• Polycount: < 2k triangles per target. (There can be 50+ on screen).
  • Hitbox: You are creating the Visual Mesh. The game engine handles the physics collision. Your mesh should fit roughly within a 0.5m x 0.5m x 0.5m cube.
  • Center Point: The mesh must be centered at (0,0,0).

docs.title: Phase 1: Modeling & Texturing
docs.text:
1. Create your assets in Blender/Maya.
  2. Export as .glb (GLTF Binary).
  3. Textures: Embed them or keep them in a relative folder.

docs.title: Phase 2: Import to SDK
docs.text:
1. Open the aerobeat-skins-* project.
  2. Drag your .glb files into the FileSystem.
  3. Double-click to open and verify materials.

docs.title: Phase 3: Configuration
docs.text:
1. Create Resource: Right-click -> New -> AeroGlove or AeroTargetSkin.
  2. Assign Mesh: Drag your imported mesh into the mesh_visual slot.
  3. Offset: Adjust position/rotation offset if the mesh doesn't align with the default hand bones.

docs.title: Phase 4: Validation & Upload
docs.text:
1. Create AeroModManifest (Type: SKIN).
  2. Open AeroBeat Uploader.
  3. Validate: Checks for polycounts and material errors or accidentally added scripts.
  4. Upload: Publish to the server.

docs.title: 🎨 Best Practices
docs.text:
• Readability: Targets move fast. Ensure the "Directional Arrow" or indicator on the target is high contrast.
  • Silhouette: Unique shapes are great, but don't make them so complex that the hit direction is ambiguous.
  • Glove Comfort: Remember the player sees the back of the glove 90% of the time. Put your best details there.

docs.title: Creating Boxing Choreography
docs.text: Boxing is a core retained AeroBeat gameplay feature and should be authored for the current camera-first product slice.

docs.title: Authoring priorities
docs.text:
• keep movement readable on camera
  • favor safe, repeatable workout motion
  • use portal placement when it improves choreography, not just because the system allows it
  • optimize first for the PC community release path

docs.title: Chart shape
docs.text:
A Boxing chart is a reusable Chart record. The song owns audio/timing, the chart owns one concrete playable difficulty, and a package-local Set links the chart to the selected song, environment, and optional coaching overlay.

Each boxing beat uses:

  • required start
  • optional inclusive end
  • required concrete type
  • optional integer portal

For current chart truth:

  • use punch_left / punch_right for straight punches, not jab / cross
  • use guard for guard holds/prompts
  • treat orthodox / southpaw as authored stance semantics, not tracked input events

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Portal-aware presentation remains valid in the docs, but it should not be read as a promise that VR or non-camera Boxing is official v1 scope.

docs.title: Creating Flow Choreography
docs.text: Flow is a retained AeroBeat gameplay feature and should be authored conservatively for the current camera-first product slice.

docs.title: Authoring priorities
docs.text:
• preserve readable continuous motion
  • keep body-movement prompts safe and well telegraphed
  • optimize for the PC-first community release path
  • treat broader platform/rendering flexibility as future-friendly, not equal-status v1 scope

docs.title: Chart shape
docs.text:
Flow charts use a flat beats: list under feature: flow.

Each beat may include:

  • required start
  • optional inclusive end
  • required type
  • optional portal
  • optional placement
  • optional direction

When both are present, keep their semantics separate:

  • placement = where the beat passes
  • direction = follow-through guidance for max-score correctness

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Flow's future adaptability across renderers and platforms is still useful, but the official product docs should stay grounded in camera-based gameplay first.

docs.title: Choreography Overview
docs.text: In AeroBeat, charting is the art of translating music into movement.

docs.title: Active choreography scope
docs.text:
The current docs set teaches choreography for the two retained gameplay features:

  • Boxing
  • Flow

Dance and Step are no longer active gameplay features in this documentation slice.

docs.title: Content model
docs.text:
AeroBeat choreography still fits the same durable chain:

  • Song → Chart → Set → Workout

docs.title: View and platform guidance
docs.text:
Creators should optimize first for the current official product slice:

  • camera-driven gameplay
  • PC-first release

Portal-aware presentation and broader platform rendering can stay in mind as future-friendly design constraints, but they should not be documented as equal-status v1 product targets.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. choose a song
  2. author or edit one Boxing or Flow chart
  3. wire that chart into a set
  4. validate readability, movement safety, and timing
  5. publish the package content through the appropriate tooling

docs.title: Refund & Cancellation Policy
docs.text:
Last Updated: [Date]

AeroBeat operates on a "Support, Don't Buy" model. Our "Supporter" tags are voluntary contributions to help fund server costs and open-source development. Because of this, our refund policy differs from traditional subscription services.

docs.title: 1. Nature of Purchase
docs.text:
• Donation-Based: Payments made for Supporter status are classified as voluntary donations with incidental benefits.
  • Non-Recurring: All purchases are for a fixed period (e.g., 1 Month, 1 Year). We do not store your card for auto-renewal. You will never be charged automatically.

docs.title: 2. General Refund Policy
docs.text:
All sales are final.

Because Supporter status provides immediate access to server-side benefits (Cloud Storage, API usage) and is treated as a donation to the project, we generally do not offer refunds for "change of mind" or "unused time".

docs.title: 3. Exceptional Circumstances
docs.text: We understand that errors happen. We will consider refund requests under the following specific circumstances:

docs.title: A. Technical Errors
docs.text: If a technical glitch caused a duplicate charge or if you were charged for a tier you did not select, we will issue a full refund for the erroneous transaction immediately.

docs.title: B. Fraudulent Activity
docs.text: If your card was used without your permission, please contact your bank to initiate a dispute, or contact us immediately to reverse the charge before it settles.

docs.title: C. Long-Term Pro-Ration (Case-by-Case)
docs.text:
For purchases of 1 Year or longer, if you are unable to use the service due to permanent hardware failure or medical reasons, you may request a pro-rated refund for the unused months.

  • Calculation: Refund = (Total Paid) - (Months Used * Monthly Rate).
  • Note: The "Months Used" are calculated at the standard $4.00/mo rate, not the discounted yearly rate.

docs.title: 4. Account Terminations
docs.text: If your account is suspended or banned due to a violation of our Code of Conduct or Terms of Service (e.g., cheating, hate speech, uploading malicious content), you forfeit all remaining Supporter time. No refunds will be issued for banned accounts.

docs.title: 5. How to Request a Refund
docs.text:
To submit a request, please email aerobeat-workouts@gmail.com with:

  1. Your Username.
  2. Transaction ID (from the receipt).
  3. Reason for the request.

We aim to respond to all billing inquiries within 48 hours.

docs.title: Licensing Overview
docs.text:
At AeroBeat, we believe in Open Source, but we also respect that the reality of Game Development involves proprietary hardware, third-party assets, and commercial partnerships.

We use a Hybrid Licensing Strategy to balance these needs. This section explains exactly what you can do, what you must share, and how to safely contribute.

docs.title: Quick Reference Table

  • "Contribution" shall mean any original work of authorship, including any modifications or additions to an existing work, that is intentionally submitted by You to AeroBeat-Workouts for inclusion in, or documentation of, any of the products owned or managed by AeroBeat-Workouts (the "Work").
  • "Submit" means any form of electronic, verbal, or written communication sent to AeroBeat-Workouts or its representatives, including but not limited to communication on electronic mailing lists, source code control systems (GitHub), and issue tracking systems.

docs.text:
1. You are legally entitled to grant the above license.
  2. Your Contribution is your original creation.
  3. Your Contribution includes complete details of any third-party license or other restriction (including, but not limited to, related patents and trademarks) of which you are personally aware and which are associated with any part of Your Contribution.

docs.title: 5. No Other Rights
docs.text: You reserve all right, title, and interest in and to Your Contributions. This CLA does not transfer ownership of your code to AeroBeat-Workouts; it only grants us a license to use it.

docs.title: 6. How to Sign
docs.text:
To sign this CLA, please add the following line to your Pull Request description or post it as a comment in the PR:

"I have read and agree to the terms of the AeroBeat-Workouts CLA."

docs.title: Contributing to AeroBeat
docs.text:
We welcome contributions of all kinds! Whether you are fixing a bug in the Core, designing a new environment, or writing an input driver for a new hardware device.

  1. Check the License: Please review our Licensing Overview to understand how your contributions will be protected.
  2. Pick a Repo: Navigate to the specific repository relevant to your skill set.
  3. Sign the CLA: To ensure we can protect the project and support commercial ports (which fund the project), we require a Contributor License Agreement for code contributions.
  4. Run Setup: Use the included ./setup_dev script to initialize your local environment.

Current Status: AeroBeat is currently in Prototype (v0.0.1). We are actively building the Core Contracts and the initial Boxing Feature.

docs.title: Attribution Guide
docs.text: If you fork AeroBeat or use our assets, you must provide proper credit. Here is how to do it correctly.

docs.title: 1. In Your Source Code
docs.text:
• Do Not Remove Headers: You must keep the existing copyright headers in all source files (GPLv3/MPL 2.0 requirement).
  • Add Your Own: If you modify a file, add your own copyright line below ours. 
    # Copyright (c) 2026 AeroBeat-Workouts
# Copyright (c) 2027 Your Name/Company

docs.title: 2. In Your Game Client
docs.text:
If you release a build (even a non-commercial one), you must credit AeroBeat in the user interface.

  • Credits Screen: Include "Powered by AeroBeat" or "Based on AeroBeat Platform".
  • Assets: If you use our CC BY-NC 4.0 assets, list "AeroBeat-Workouts" in your art credits.

docs.title: For Commercial Partners
docs.text: While our community releases are strictly GPLv3, AeroBeat-Workouts retains the copyright to the codebase. This allows us to offer Dual Licensing for commercial partners who need to bypass the restrictions of the GPL.

docs.title: What is Dual Licensing?
docs.text:
Dual Licensing means the same code is available under two different licenses:

  1. Open Source (GPLv3): Free to use, but requires you to share your source code if you distribute it.
  2. Commercial (Proprietary): A paid license that allows you to keep your source code private, modify the engine freely, and deploy to closed platforms (like Consoles or Arcades).

docs.title: Why would I need a Commercial License?
docs.text:
• Closed Source Requirements: You are building a proprietary game (e.g., "Linkin Park Arcade Edition") and cannot share the source code for your specific gameplay logic or UI Shell.
  • Console NDAs: Sony, Nintendo, and Microsoft SDKs are under NDA and cannot be mixed with GPL open-source code repositories.
  • Legal Safety: You want a traditional software license agreement with indemnification, rather than relying on a community license.

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
Because all contributors sign a Contributor License Agreement (CLA), AeroBeat-Workouts has the legal right to re-license the codebase.

  1. Contact Us: Reach out to discuss your project scope.
  2. License Grant: We provide a specific license grant for the repositories you need (e.g., aerobeat-assembly-*, aerobeat-ui-shell-*).
  3. Development: You fork the repositories privately. You can pull updates from the Open Source upstream, but you are not required to push your changes back.
  4. Funding: The licensing fees go directly into funding the core development team and maintaining the Open Source version.

docs.title: Commercial FAQ
docs.text:
Q: Can I sell a game built on AeroBeat?

A: Yes. However, if you use the free Community Edition (GPLv3), you must release your game's source code to your players. If you wish to keep your source code private, you must purchase a Commercial License.

Q: Can I release on Consoles (Switch, PS5, Xbox)?

A: Not with the Community Edition. Console SDKs are protected by NDAs that are legally incompatible with the GPLv3 license. You need a Commercial License to legally deploy AeroBeat to consoles.

Q: Can I use the default songs and skins in my commercial game?

A: No. The content in our Art repositories (aerobeat-skins-*, aerobeat-avatars-*, etc.) is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 (Non-Commercial). You must replace these assets with your own art and music, or contact us to license specific assets.

Q: Does AeroBeat take a revenue cut?

A: The Open Source version is free. Commercial Licenses are negotiated based on the project scope (Flat fee or Royalty).

Q: I am a hardware vendor. Do I need a commercial license to write a driver?

A: No. Input drivers use MPL 2.0, which allows linking to proprietary SDKs. You can write and distribute a driver for your hardware without paying us or opening your SDK source code.

docs.title: For Creatives
docs.text: Our creative content is protected differently than our code to prevent "Asset Flips."

docs.title: Content Packs (Skins, Avatars, Environments)
docs.text:
• License: CC BY-NC 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial)
  • Can I use these assets for fun? Yes! You can remix them, mod them, and share them.
  • Can I use them in a commercial game? No. You cannot take our 3D models, textures, or music and sell them in your own product.
  • Can I claim I made them? No. You must give credit to AeroBeat-Workouts.

docs.title: Documentation (aerobeat-docs)
docs.text:
• License: CC BY-NC 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial)
  • The Rule: Our Wiki, GDD, and Architecture docs are community-owned. If you improve them, you must share those improvements under the same license. You cannot use our documentation for commercial means.

docs.title: For Software Engineers
docs.text: Our codebase is split into three categories. The license depends on which repository you are touching.

docs.title: 1. The Shared Core Lanes (aerobeat-input-core, aerobeat-feature-core, aerobeat-content-core, aerobeat-asset-core, aerobeat-ui-core, aerobeat-tool-core)
docs.text:
• License: Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
  • The Rule: If you modify these files, you must share your changes.
  • The Nuance: This is "File-Level Copyleft." You can link proprietary modules to these shared cores (for example a closed-source SDK wrapped behind an input or tool integration) as long as the core files themselves remain open.

docs.title: 2. The Game Client (aerobeat-assembly-* & aerobeat-feature-*)
docs.text:
• License: GNU GPLv3
  • The Rule: The "Product" is strictly Open Source. If you distribute a modified version of the AeroBeat Game Client, you must release the entire source code.
  • Why: This prevents corporations from taking our community's work, closing the source, and selling it as a clone.

docs.title: 3. Hardware Drivers (aerobeat-input-*)
docs.text:
• License: Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
  • The Rule: Input drivers are treated like the Engine Core.
  • Why: We want hardware vendors (e.g., Smart Watch makers, VR Headset companies) to write official drivers for AeroBeat. MPL 2.0 allows them to link their proprietary SDKs (DLLs/Libs) to our Input System without violating the license.

docs.title: 4. The UI Stack (aerobeat-ui-*)
docs.text:
• UI Core & Kits (aerobeat-ui-core, aerobeat-ui-kit-*): Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
    • The Rule: These are treated as standard libraries. You can link them to proprietary shells if needed, but changes to the files themselves must be shared.
  • UI Shells (aerobeat-ui-shell-*): GNU GPLv3
    • The Rule: The Shell contains the specific application flow and wiring. It is treated as part of the Game Client and must be open source.
    • Why: This prevents bad actors from taking the community-built interface, rebranding it as their own, and closing the source.

docs.title: 5. Shared Tools (aerobeat-tool-*)
docs.text:
• License: Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
  • The Rule: Treated as libraries.
  • Why: These are generic services (API clients, Analytics) that can be used in proprietary forks without infecting the application logic, provided file-level changes are shared.

docs.title: For Hardware Partners
docs.text:
If you are a hardware vendor looking to integrate your device (Smart Glove, Haptic Vest, XR Headset) with AeroBeat:

  1. You are safe. Our Input Tier (aerobeat-input-*) uses MPL 2.0.
  2. No Viral Infection. You can create a repository aerobeat-input-yourbrand that contains a mix of Open Source GDScript and your Closed Source SDK/DLLs.
  3. Linking is Allowed. As long as you do not modify the AeroBeat Core files themselves, your proprietary logic remains yours.
